concepts.title: Why the Croatian alphabet is your superpower
concepts.simpleExplanation: Croatian spelling is perfectly phonetic: every letter is always pronounced the same way, and every sound is always written the same way. There are no silent letters and no surprises. That means once you've learned the 30 letters — especially the special ones like č, ć, š, ž, dž, đ, lj and nj — you can correctly read aloud ANY Croatian word you've never seen, and you can spell any word you hear. English speakers spend years on spelling; in Croatian you solve it in a week.
concepts.analogy: It's like a piano where each key always plays the same note. Learn the keys once and you can play any sheet of music on sight.

rubricPoints.point: Croatian is 100% phonetic.
rubricPoints.reTeach: Each letter always makes the same sound, and each sound is always written the same way.

rubricPoints.point: There are no silent letters.
rubricPoints.reTeach: Unlike French or English, you pronounce everything you see.

rubricPoints.point: Special letters (č/ć/š/ž/dž/đ/lj/nj) must be learned.
rubricPoints.reTeach: These carry sounds like ch, sh, zh, ly, ny — learn them and you've cracked pronunciation.

rubricPoints.point: Once learned, you can read AND spell any word.
rubricPoints.reTeach: Reading aloud and spelling-from-listening both become automatic.

concepts.title: Why Croatian has no 'a' or 'the'
concepts.simpleExplanation: Croatian simply doesn't have articles. There is no word for 'a' and no word for 'the'. A noun like 'kuća' can mean 'a house', 'the house', or just 'house' depending on context. This makes sentences shorter, but it means you have to rely on the situation (and sometimes word order or demonstratives like 'ovaj' = this) to know which English article fits when translating.
concepts.analogy: Imagine English if you just said 'I see house' and let the conversation make clear whether it's a specific house or any house. That's Croatian.

rubricPoints.point: There is no 'a' and no 'the'.
rubricPoints.reTeach: Croatian has zero articles — none at all.

rubricPoints.point: A bare noun covers a/the/none.
rubricPoints.reTeach: 'kuća' = a house / the house / house, depending on context.

rubricPoints.point: Context (or words like 'ovaj') supplies definiteness.
rubricPoints.reTeach: If you need to say 'this/that house', use demonstratives like ovaj/taj.

concepts.title: What grammatical cases actually do
concepts.simpleExplanation: In Croatian, the ENDING of a noun changes depending on the job it does in the sentence. These different endings are called cases. The nominative is the dictionary form and marks the subject (the one doing the action). The accusative marks the direct object (the thing receiving the action). So 'žena' (woman, subject) drinks 'kavu' (coffee, object) — 'kava' changed its ending to 'kavu' to show it's the object. Because the ending tells you each word's role, word order can move around without losing the meaning. Croatian has 7 cases, but nominative and accusative alone handle most basic sentences.
concepts.analogy: Cases are like name tags that say 'I'm the subject' or 'I'm the object'. Once everyone wears a tag, they can stand anywhere in the room and you still know who's who.

rubricPoints.point: A case is shown by the noun's ENDING.
rubricPoints.reTeach: The end of the word changes to show its role — not a separate word, the ending itself.

rubricPoints.point: Nominative = the subject (dictionary form).
rubricPoints.reTeach: The doer of the action, and the form you look up: 'žena'.

rubricPoints.point: Accusative = the direct object.
rubricPoints.reTeach: The receiver of the action: 'kava' → 'kavu' in 'Pijem kavu'.

rubricPoints.point: Because endings show role, word order is flexible.
rubricPoints.reTeach: You can rearrange words and the case endings keep the meaning clear.

rubricPoints.point: There are 7 cases; start with just nominative + accusative.
rubricPoints.reTeach: Don't learn all 7 at once — these two cover most basic sentences.

concepts.title: Verb aspect: ongoing vs completed
concepts.simpleExplanation: Most Croatian verbs come in pairs that describe the SAME action but from two viewpoints. The imperfective verb views the action as ongoing, repeated, or a process ('I was drinking', 'I drink every day'). The perfective verb views it as a single, completed whole ('I drank it all up'). For example, 'piti' is imperfective and 'popiti' is perfective. Often a prefix (like po-, na-, pro-) turns an imperfective verb into its perfective partner. Aspect replaces a lot of the work English does with different tenses.
concepts.analogy: Imperfective is a video of the action playing; perfective is a photo of it finished. Same event, different lens.

rubricPoints.point: Verbs come in imperfective/perfective pairs.
rubricPoints.reTeach: Two verbs describe the same action from different viewpoints.

rubricPoints.point: Imperfective = ongoing/repeated/process.
rubricPoints.reTeach: Use it for actions in progress or habits: 'I was reading', 'I read daily'.

rubricPoints.point: Perfective = a single completed action.
rubricPoints.reTeach: Use it for a finished whole: 'I read (and finished) the book'.

rubricPoints.point: A prefix often makes a verb perfective.
rubricPoints.reTeach: piti → popiti, pisati → napisati: the prefix signals completion.

concepts.title: How the past tense (perfekt) works
concepts.simpleExplanation: The everyday Croatian past tense is built from two pieces: the short form of biti ('to be' — sam, si, je, smo, ste, su) plus a special past participle of the main verb. The participle changes its ending to agree with the speaker's gender and number: -o for masculine, -la for feminine, -lo for neuter, -li/-le for plural. So a man says 'Radio sam' (I worked) and a woman says 'Radila sam'. To make it negative, you swap to the negative biti: 'Nisam radio' (I didn't work).
concepts.analogy: It's like English 'I have worked', but the 'worked' part puts on a different outfit depending on whether a man, woman, or group is speaking.

rubricPoints.point: Past = short biti + past participle.
rubricPoints.reTeach: Two pieces: sam/si/je... plus the participle, e.g. 'sam' + 'radio'.

rubricPoints.point: The participle agrees with gender and number.
rubricPoints.reTeach: -o (m), -la (f), -lo (n), -li/-le (pl).

rubricPoints.point: Man: 'Radio sam'; woman: 'Radila sam'.
rubricPoints.reTeach: The same action takes a different participle ending by speaker gender.

rubricPoints.point: Negative uses negative biti (nisam...).
rubricPoints.reTeach: 'Nisam radio' = I didn't work — swap the helper to its negative form.

concepts.title: The genitive — the workhorse third case
concepts.simpleExplanation: After nominative and accusative, the genitive is the case you'll meet most. It does several common jobs: it shows possession and 'of' ('a glass OF water', 'my brother's car'), it appears after many prepositions (od, do, iz, bez = from, to, out of, without), it's used after most numbers and quantity words ('a lot OF books'), and it marks the thing that doesn't exist after 'nema' ('there is no...'). Masculine and neuter nouns typically add -a; feminine -a nouns change to -e.
concepts.analogy: If nominative and accusative are 'who does what to whom', the genitive is the 'of' glue that connects nouns together and the case that prepositions and quantities reach for.

rubricPoints.point: Genitive shows possession and 'of'.
rubricPoints.reTeach: 'čaša vode' = a glass of water; 'auto mog brata' = my brother's car.

rubricPoints.point: It follows many prepositions (od, do, iz, bez).
rubricPoints.reTeach: 'iz grada' = out of the city, 'bez problema' = without a problem.

rubricPoints.point: It's used after numbers and quantity words.
rubricPoints.reTeach: 'puno knjiga' = a lot of books; quantities take the genitive.

rubricPoints.point: It marks the absent thing after 'nema'.
rubricPoints.reTeach: 'Nema vode' = there is no water — the missing thing goes in the genitive.

rubricPoints.point: Endings: masc/neut add -a; fem -a → -e.
rubricPoints.reTeach: grad → grada, more → mora, žena → žene.

concepts.title: Why prepositions decide the case
concepts.simpleExplanation: In Croatian a preposition doesn't just sit in front of a noun — it dictates which case that noun must take. Each preposition 'governs' a fixed case: 'iz' (from) and 'od' (from/of) always take the genitive, 's' (with) takes the instrumental, 'k' (towards) takes the dative. The tricky ones are prepositions like 'u' (in/into) and 'na' (on/onto) that take TWO cases depending on meaning: the accusative for motion towards ('idem u grad' = I go INTO the city) and the locative for a static location ('živim u gradu' = I live IN the city). So to pick the right ending you ask two questions: which preposition, and is there motion or rest?
concepts.analogy: Think of each preposition as a vending machine that only accepts one type of coin (case). A few machines ('u', 'na') accept two coins — and which one depends on whether you're moving or staying put.

rubricPoints.point: A preposition forces a specific case on its noun.
rubricPoints.reTeach: The noun's ending is decided by the preposition in front of it.

rubricPoints.point: Fixed examples: iz/od → genitive, s → instrumental, k → dative.
rubricPoints.reTeach: Memorise which case each common preposition governs.

rubricPoints.point: 'u' and 'na' take accusative for motion, locative for location.
rubricPoints.reTeach: 'u grad' (into, accusative) vs 'u gradu' (in, locative).

rubricPoints.point: Ask: which preposition, and motion or rest?
rubricPoints.reTeach: Those two questions pick the correct case ending every time.

concepts.title: Second-position clitics — the little words with strict seats
concepts.simpleExplanation: Croatian has a set of short, unstressed words — auxiliary forms (sam, si, je, ću...), the question word 'li', the reflexive 'se', and short pronouns (me, te, ga, mu, joj...). These clitics can't stand just anywhere: they must sit in the SECOND position of the clause, right after the first stressed word or phrase. And when several appear together they line up in a fixed internal order: li → auxiliary (sam/će...) → 'se' → dative pronoun → accusative pronoun → 'je'. So 'I have already given it to her' is 'Već sam joj ga dao' — after 'Već', the chain sam-joj-ga falls into place. Getting this rhythm right is what makes Croatian sound native.
concepts.analogy: The clitics are like train carriages that must couple in a fixed order and always hook onto the first word of the sentence — you can't scatter them down the platform.

rubricPoints.point: Clitics are short, unstressed words (sam, se, ga, mu, li...).
rubricPoints.reTeach: Auxiliaries, 'se', 'li', and short pronouns are all clitics.

rubricPoints.point: They go in SECOND position, after the first stressed word.
rubricPoints.reTeach: 'Već sam...' / 'Dao sam...' — the clitic follows the opening word.

rubricPoints.point: Multiple clitics follow a fixed internal order.
rubricPoints.reTeach: li → aux → se → dative → accusative → je.

rubricPoints.point: Correct clitic order is what sounds native.
rubricPoints.reTeach: 'Već sam joj ga dao' — practise the chain until it's automatic.

concepts.title: What makes Croatian distinctly Croatian (ijekavian)
concepts.simpleExplanation: Croatian, Serbian, Bosnian and Montenegrin are largely mutually intelligible, but standard Croatian has consistent features that mark it out. The biggest is the 'ijekavian' reflex of an old vowel: where Serbian (ekavian) says 'mleko', 'dete', 'lepo', Croatian says 'mlijeko', 'dijete', 'lijepo'. Croatian also prefers certain native words and word-formation patterns (e.g. 'tisuća' for thousand, '-irati' verbs, native month names like 'siječanj' rather than 'januar'). At C1, command means producing this reflex and lexis consistently, and adjusting register — knowing when a colloquial or dialectal form is fine and when the polished standard is expected.
concepts.analogy: It's like the difference between British and American English: the same language, but a consistent set of spellings, words and rhythms signals which standard you're speaking — and a polished speaker stays consistent.

rubricPoints.point: Croatian uses the ijekavian reflex (mlijeko, dijete, lijepo).
rubricPoints.reTeach: Where ekavian has 'e', Croatian typically has 'ije'/'je': mleko→mlijeko.

rubricPoints.point: It favours certain native words and forms.
rubricPoints.reTeach: e.g. 'tisuća' (thousand), native month names like 'siječanj'.

rubricPoints.point: Consistency in this reflex/lexis signals polished Croatian.
rubricPoints.reTeach: Producing ijekavian forms reliably is a C1 marker.

rubricPoints.point: C1 also means controlling register and dialect deliberately.
rubricPoints.reTeach: Know when colloquial/dialect is fine vs when the standard is expected.
